podcasts.name: Training Data
podcasts.title: How Cursor Trained Composer on Fireworks: Distributed Infrastructure for High-Performance RL
podcasts.transcript:
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:20
You need all the infrastructure to run these environments that have to mimic as closely as possible with a user as a computer would look like. And it's very important as closely as possible because sometimes the model can actually figure out when it's being run-in like a fake environment or not a real one, and it has like different behaviors during RL than in production.

Speaker 2 | 00:20 - 00:26
Are you seeing it being conscious that it's being it's in a fake environment and starts being behaving differently?

Speaker 1 | 00:26 - 00:34
Yes. Yes. Interesting. Like, it's like, oh, I'm in a fake environment. I've learned a few tricks to, like, get a better reward in this environment, and let me try them out.

Speaker 3 | 00:34 - 00:37
Models love to cheat. IRL is really good at encouraging cheating.

Speaker 2 | 00:53 - 01:26
I'm delighted to welcome Federico from Cursor and Dima from Fireworks to the podcast today. Federico, you are the research lead on Composer two at Cursor, Cursor's new agentic coding model. And, Dima, you spent how many of the last few months moonlighting at Cursor in order to support a lot of the infrastructure required to make this gargantuan training task happen. And so I'm excited to talk to both of you today about how the training of Composer two came together, what hard problems you solved together, and what do you think it means for the future of of AI and foundation model companies.

Speaker 3 | 01:26 - 01:28
Exciting. Yeah. Exciting. Thank you for having us.

Speaker 2 | 01:28 - 01:47
Thanks for joining. Okay. Let's dive right in. For those who haven't been following us closely, Cursor recently announced Composer two, which is an agentic coding model, meant for long horizon coding tasks. Federico, up till now, Cursor was mostly, enabling, other people's, coding agents.

Speaker 2 | 01:47 - 01:57
What was the impetus for Cursor to lean so heavily into Composer two? And how existential is it for you to become not just an application company, but also a foundation model company yourselves?

Speaker 1 | 01:57 - 02:23
The reason why we started the looking into training our own models is you can sort of think about the model as sort of like like a storage drive. It has certain amount of bits that it can store in its weights. And the the idea is very simple, you know, like, care about only one task. We don't even care about coding or programming necessarily. We care about software engineering inside cursor and inside the cursor only.

Speaker 1 | 02:23 - 02:51
And so what if we were to allocate all of the bits of information that can be stored inside a model weights to that one particular task? Also, as people may have noticed, composer is order of magnitude, the less expensive than opus and other like coding models because we can just simply specialize all of the model weights to that particular task. And so we can serve like a smaller model or something of that sort. Yeah.

Speaker 2 | 02:51 - 02:57
So it's about let's make sure every single bit of weight or information we have is dedicated toward the specific problem that we have at hand.

Speaker 1 | 02:57 - 02:57
Exactly.

Speaker 2 | 02:57 - 03:08
Got it. That seems like it's an almost generalizable problem. DiEM, I'm curious of your perspective. Do you think that every application company should be looking at Cursor as a harbinger of what's to come? Like, should they all be looking to do the same thing?

Speaker 3 | 03:08 - 03:38
Yeah. Absolutely. I mean, we actually generally see it as a pattern of kind of evolution of web applications. You maybe start prototyping, or you might be using kind of off the shelf model to get something running, maybe do some prompt engineering, figure out how your hardness works. But the most kind of leveraged attribute of your application is actual usage of user data or particular specific aspects of how the application works, maybe some aspects of your harness, which tools do you provide, how the application works, kind of really important bits which are important for your application.

Speaker 3 | 03:38 - 03:45
And the right way to capture that, you can do a little bit of that through prompting. But really, the right way to do this is craft your model to act in your environment.

Speaker 1 | 03:45 - 04:15
Yeah. Absolutely. Like, are certain tools the agent calls that it's very hard to succinctly describe exactly the behavior of that tool to the model. And, you know, with just like post training, we can bake in the optimal way to use those tools. Like composer, we we do serve a prompt to composer, but I I think the way we are training it, it would work even without a prompt, and it would know what to do just because, like, we are intrinsically pushing the model to, like, the right direction of how it should act throughout our training.

Speaker 1 | 04:15 - 04:16
Basically, there's kinda,

Speaker 3 | 04:16 - 04:35
like, upper bound of, like, how far you can get this prompt engineering. And if you want to craft really great AI products, you have to go through kind of and influencing model behavior. That's kind of one reason. I mean, reason number two is what the recommendation is kind of cost trade off or, like, speed trade off. Like, the way we kind of view it as Fireworks is that that, you know, trying to do optimization.

Speaker 3 | 04:36 - 04:57
You had this, like, three-dimensional trade off between quality, speed, and cost. And you can go quite far, and we are doing it with all the customers. Initially, we can go quite far with just optimizing infrastructure. But when you start getting to model training, can really push this trade off much further, and you can get better model at fraction of the cost running much faster. And, you know, Composer is a great example of

Speaker 2 | 04:57 - 05:20
Can I push on this a little bit? I wanna ask if this approach is bitter lesson pills. And we were we were actually all talking about tab nine on the walk in. I'm remembering before the LLM era, there were these, like, small specialized coding models. And one of the things that was, I think, surprising to to a lot of people was as you've scaled up, you know, you scaled up just training on the Internet and a lot a bunch of English texts and other languages.

Speaker 2 | 05:20 - 05:33
Actually, the models themselves got inherently better at coding as well. And so at least the trend line I've seen so far is just like bigger models perform better on everything, including on coding. Is what you guys are saying does that go against the the grain of the bitter lesson?

Speaker 1 | 05:33 - 06:02
I think no. But one one sort of, like, thing to point out is that the big models trained by the labs train on a lot of code as well. Like, code is one of the main tasks the labs are interested in pushing, and so they don't just generalize to it. They're a bit specialized as well. I think for our case, actually, you know, if we believe about the bitter lesson, we are just pushing very hard on the data dimension, and we know that the models inherently have finite capacity.

Speaker 1 | 06:02 - 06:14
And so if we wanna saturate all that capacity, we need to scale data. And in order to ingest more data, we we need to, like, free up the weights from distractions the model may have.

Speaker 2 | 06:14 - 06:19
Okay. Got it. Super interesting. Okay. Let's dig into the training of Composer two.

Speaker 2 | 06:19 - 06:30
You launched a couple weeks ago, immediately grabbed attention. Strong benchmark numbers, much lower cost to to run inference on. What's the short version of how Composer two works and and what you guys did to make it so performant?

Speaker 1 | 06:30 - 06:56
We started from a very strong base, which is Kymi 2.5. That's like a 1,000,000,000,000 m parameter MOE that's 30 b active. So very, very sparse actually. We sort of like looked at the stock and realized there are like two axes. So mainly Composer one was just pushing on one of these axes, which is reinforcement learning, but composer two pushes in two different axes.

Speaker 1 | 06:56 - 07:22
One is continual pre training and the other is reinforcement learning. So the thing that made composer two very good is pushing in both of these directions. So we started off the training ground by doing lots of mid training on code tokens, almost sort of pre training scale actually. And then coming out of that mid training ground, we took the checkpoints and we did very large scale RL on lots of lots of tasks.

Speaker 2 | 07:23 - 07:35
Okay. And then the premise here would be because Cursor sits in the middle of so many interesting coding tokens, you actually pretty uniquely have access to data to be able to train at almost pretraining scale. Yeah. Why not pretrain your own model then?

Speaker 1 | 07:35 - 08:15
We just think about our approach from top down instead of bottom up. So like, how do we get a model that's useful to users in the least time possible if we were to start from the bottom, of figure out how how we do pre training and then scale it up to mid training and then, okay, now we figured out mid training, we do reinforcement learning, that would take a very long time to get a model out to our users. By doing it the other way around, we were able to give our useful model to our users in very little time. So hopefully, you know, like, next composer versions are gonna be our own model instead of basing it off on open source base.

Speaker 2 | 08:15 - 08:22
And what is the model roughly learning in the kind of mid training step? What is the model learning in the post training step for you?

Speaker 1 | 08:22 - 08:54
Yeah. So in mid training, it's sort of just kind of learning about libraries of code and learning about specific code patterns that are very common, like just world knowledge as well. There is like web data there as well. And this is sort of just creating a wider distribution that then reinforcement learning can sharpen on. And so during reinforcement learning, you know, the model gets to play directly with the cursor harness, and so it gets to learn about the world the model is gonna live in for the rest of its life, right, in in some way.

Speaker 1 | 08:54 - 09:27
And and so then during reinforcement learning, that's where it learns how to call tools properly, how to navigate its environment, how to write correct code. Because during mid training, it it learns how to write code. That doesn't necessarily mean it learns how to write correct code. We try to train on code that is largely only correct, but the model doesn't actually know how to differentiate between the two. While in RL, one of the key things that we are doing is we're kind of tuning the feature of the model saying, hey, now you gotta right, correct code all the time.

Speaker 1 | 09:27 - 09:27
Exactly.

Speaker 2 | 09:27 - 09:36
Interesting. And is the is the model after mid training, is that similar to the model you guys have on tab auto complete, or is that a different core competency?

Speaker 1 | 09:36 - 09:47
Yeah. I mean, it's yeah. That think I would put it like that because, like, during mid training, we are just doing the next token prediction, you know, like how well you predict the next token and then the token after that. So yeah.

Speaker 2 | 09:47 - 09:50
So why not just post train on your tab on a complete model then? Why mid trains the different model?

Speaker 1 | 09:50 - 10:06
Yeah. I mean, tab is a very small model because it's like a super low latency model as we want it to be very fast. So like the core two distinctions about the base models here is that tab is like small and and composer is quite large.

Speaker 2 | 10:06 - 10:16
I see. I see. Okay. So it seems like a lot of the focus of what you guys did for Composer two was this large scale reinforcement learning run. Can you break that down for us?

Speaker 2 | 10:16 - 10:20
Like, what goes into that, and what are the various hard problems you solve along the way?

Speaker 3 | 10:20 - 10:53
When you do RL, it's quite different from, like, pre training or retraining, because you're not just trying to predict next token. You're actually running the entire harness, like, the entire experiment. You're letting the model act in the environment, see how how it performs for a given rollout. That's the terminology which is called rollout, and kind of assign it to reward whether it did something correctly or not, which might be some l using LM as a judge or maybe something verifiable, like does this code compile or something like this, which actually means that compared to this regular training, would need a bunch of other components. Like, you still need large scale training.

Speaker 3 | 10:53 - 11:11
You still need to orchestrate tens of thousands of GPUs to the forward backward propagation, do all the stuff you do in mid training and pre training. But now you also need to orchestrate a bunch of environments. You need to run model inference because when you do this rollout, you're effectively running like real Courser session in some sense, right? So if you have your

Speaker 2 | 11:11 - 11:13
So rollout is like a forward pass.

Speaker 3 | 11:13 - 11:26
No, rollout is basically your entire agent session from Courser, right? So we basically means it might take something like 50 turns. Model will take your initial prompt, then decide to call some tools. You want to execute those tools. Then model generates a bunch of other code Yeah.

Speaker 3 | 11:26 - 12:00
Kind of entire session which you, when you interact with agent and cursor, right? You kind of simulate this entire session as a part of your training run. You get to final rewards and use that signal to now go back to trainer and incorporate it in the model weights. So you have this kind of very big update loop, which is very heterogeneous, right, because you have all these different components working together. And now you're trying to orchestrate all of this to work efficiently and work with high throughputs because GPUs are expensive, and you want to get your model trained quickly in an economic fashion.

Speaker 3 | 12:00 - 12:24
So that's, by by itself, is, like, very interesting kind of problem on intersection of algorithms and infrastructure, because there are a lot of trade offs how you can kind of co optimize and co design the system. One aspect is kind of people call about, like, a of a pipeline URL. The idea is basically, okay, you're trying to update this model in steps. Right? So you have your current model version, and you are trying to do a bunch of rollouts with it.

Speaker 3 | 12:24 - 12:38
What does your trainer do while you're doing these rollouts? Right? Like, naive approach would say that, okay, now I'm gonna stop my trainer. I'm gonna do a bunch of sessions, and those sessions might run for, five, ten minutes or even longer if it's, like, longer horizon tasks. I'm gonna get those outcomes.

Speaker 3 | 12:38 - 12:56
And now I'm gonna pause my inference. I mean, I go back to training, trying to do updates. That's, like, very theoretically, algorithmically robust because you are not precisely simulating everything. But it's very system inefficient because half of your capacity is sitting idle as time. So you can do all the clever, like, algorithmic tricks, allowing

Speaker 2 | 12:56 - 12:57
You need to to instead. Yeah.

Speaker 3 | 12:57 - 13:06
You you can like kind of pipeline all of this. So imagine this as a gigantic, like, factory. Right? You have this, like, trainer building, and you have rollouts building. They're always churning, right?

Speaker 3 | 13:06 - 13:45
So rollouts always take latest model version and try to do new sessions and kind of simulate new agent sessions. And trainer always takes new outcomes as they come and try to compute updates. So everything is moving along all the time. The trade off is that why I'm saying that algorithmically it's different, because now by the time you finish some test rollouts in your kind of simulated environment, maybe model weights already updated on some other data. So you have this kind of staleness, like, delay between how quickly model can learn updates, because by the time you kind of process for some interaction session with the simulated environment, your model base changed.

Speaker 3 | 13:45 - 14:03
And that introduces interesting training dynamics. And there are clever ways how you can address this. But the flip side of that is that your all your GPUs, all your computers kind of loaded and chime in all the time, which actually you're using more more flops and to your bitter less an example. Yeah. You you have, like, higher compute efficiency.

Speaker 3 | 14:03 - 14:16
You can get to a better model in smaller amount of time. Yeah. Maybe you're losing a few percent from being asynchronous and not doing, like, perfect mathematical updates. Yeah. But you way compensate for that by effectively not leaving half your capacity on the table.

Speaker 3 | 14:16 - 14:20
And there are a lot of kind of depths and interest in interaction in that part.

Speaker 1 | 14:20 - 14:47
And we're very serious about performance at Cursor because unlike the big labs, you know, we have tens of thousands of GPUs, not millions. And so, yeah, we do all sorts of tricks to make get the most out of GPU. Like, we train in production with f before even. We work with fireworks to to, like, push on inference as well. Because the thing about RL infrastructure is just like it's just inherently more complex than pretraining because you need all the pretraining infrastructure.

Speaker 1 | 14:47 - 15:10
That's just like one of the requirements. Then you need all the infrastructure to run these environments that have to mimic as closely as possible what a user as a computer would look like. And it's very important as closely as possible because sometimes the model can actually figure out when it's being run-in like a fake environment or not a real one, and it has like different behaviors during RL than in production.

Speaker 2 | 15:11 - 15:16
Are you seeing it being conscious that it's being it's in a fake environment, it starts being behaving differently?

Speaker 3 | 15:16 - 15:18
Yes. Yes. Interesting.

Speaker 1 | 15:18 - 15:24
Like, it's like, oh, I'm in a fake environment. I've learned a few tricks to, like, get a better reward in this environment, and let me try them out.

Speaker 3 | 15:24 - 15:27
Models love to cheat. ARAL is really good at encouraging cheating.

Speaker 1 | 15:28 - 15:39
Yeah. Yeah. And then we need a really efficient inference. So this is really important. So there is like actually this kind of myth that during GARAL you spend more, way more inference flops than training flops.

Speaker 1 | 15:39 - 16:02
This is sort of like just because the open source inference engines are very unoptimized instead of actually being a property of RL. Roughly the same ratio is kind of the same. In theory, if you push the GPUs to the maximum, you should have one third of your training GPUs allocated to inference. Right? Because training is effectively three forward passes.

Speaker 1 | 16:02 - 16:12
You have the forward pass, you have the data gradient, the weight gradient. While if you really hit the critical batch size on inference, you should only have a single forward password of flops.

Speaker 2 | 16:12 - 16:16
So that's why you guys use fireworks instead of using an open inference engine?

Speaker 1 | 16:16 - 16:31
Yeah. I mean, the other alternative is we would build one in house, but you know, we have finite engineers like everybody else. We would, like, prefer to have engineers make training more efficient and more precise rather than, like, spin up like inference effort. Yeah.

Speaker 2 | 16:31 - 16:40
Okay. That's super hardcore. What about thinking you mentioned in your technical paper paper that you were doing this in a kind of globally distributed way. Why globally distributed? And then what makes that hard?

Speaker 1 | 16:40 - 16:55
Yeah. Yeah. Well, there are various reasons. One, you know, like this very large contiguous clusters are harder to find in the market. And so what we can do instead is we have one cluster that's gonna run all of training.

Speaker 1 | 16:55 - 17:30
You know, we can't do global training cluster. But then the inference component of reinforcement learning, we can globally distribute that across small clusters all over the world. So I think for the composer to run, we use the four clusters in total that were all over the world, very far away from each other. And we even used some of our production traffic when it was least used. So like we had the Composer 1.5, the previous model served, and when it was least used by people, we just grabbed some inference GPUs and we put them to speed up training.

Speaker 1 | 17:30 - 17:41
And so we can do these sort of things and sort of easily scale up our training ground without having one large contiguous cluster. And the thing that enables it, maybe Vidima can talk more I mean,

Speaker 3 | 17:41 - 18:00
kinda like to to reframeulate what Federico said is basically, RL training is, like, very heterogeneous. Right? And by leveraging heterogeneity, how different components like, what infrastructures they need, you can actually drive efficiency. And you see this pattern kind of across the board everywhere. Specifically for for training, you have all these, like, highly interconnected clusters.

Speaker 3 | 18:00 - 18:09
You need high speed network, kind of need to work in lockstep. So those clusters are expensive. Right? And actually, it's really hard to find big ones. Right?

Speaker 3 | 18:09 - 18:32
Basically, at the scale at which Composer was trained, finding like 2x larger cluster is like significantly harder than finding the current size one. And that's why if you can disaggregate these components and put them on different places, one, you don't need to find such a big cluster. Two, you can actually find different trade offs of hardware. Because for inference, you don't need that of wide interconnect. You can have smaller groups of GPUs interconnected together.

Speaker 3 | 18:32 - 18:45
You can have heterogeneous types of GPUs. You can have different generations of GPUs. You can kind of play all these games games optimization. And finally, like inference, it's much easier to scale up and down as you go. And, yeah, it's very conventional.

Speaker 3 | 18:45 - 19:08
Like, when you have off peak hours, you can view all your kind of inference pool as one set of GPUs. Serving production traffic for real users or serving simulated environments for RL purposes and kind of balance between this. Of course, it's a very interesting systems problem. So you can mention, like, the Kymi model is, like, one one terabyte. Training step takes somewhere between, like, five to fifteen minutes.

Speaker 3 | 19:08 - 19:27
So it basically means, like, every, like, every five to ten minutes, you are producing, like, one terabyte new snapshot of weights. So the question is, like, how are you gonna ship it to a different cluster on this other side of the world very efficiently. Right? And you want to, like, do it quickly because remember, you don't want to get this staleness to get out of hand. So I think that was probably one Yeah.

Speaker 3 | 19:27 - 19:48
The the kind of the the most fun part which we figured out together is that despite, you know, full full model being like one terabyte, not all the weights change every step. Right? Because RL does a lot of very, like, precise adjustments, especially as the training going on. So actually, there are very kind of regular patterns in, like, which subset of weights gets changed. Maybe not all of them change every time.

Speaker 3 | 19:49 - 20:12
So if you were to look at, like, how my model changes within one training step, like, after ten minutes, there is relatively small delta between those. You can write write a compression algorithm, which basically leverages this property. And now you end up with kinda like database systems problem, which is, okay. I have my delta, and I just want to, like, ship it across across the world. My delta might be, like, 20 times smaller than what ship in the full model is.

Speaker 3 | 20:12 - 20:33
And that and that makes it practical. But, of course, now you need to build all this kind of machinery from storage systems of full snapshots and deltas and recovery and reconciliation, etcetera. We were able to build it kind of in lossless fashion. Basically means that you always end up with bit equivalent model on the other side. So you don't need to worry about any mass aspects of this, and you can do it really fast too.

Speaker 3 | 20:33 - 20:44
You can you you can do it under, you know, under a few minutes. Even in the worst conditions, usually, it's under a minute. And most importantly, you, like, pause only for, like, maybe thirty seconds to swap the weights in your actual inference server.

Speaker 1 | 20:44 - 20:51
To, like, fully, like, saturated the band the egress of the cluster by, like, sharding the upload and the download as well. So you

Speaker 3 | 20:51 - 21:15
can do all these, like, system tricks to print the stand down. It is it is quite a few complexity, but you can kind of abstract it out and just make it work great. Like, it doesn't interfere with your training algorithm. And on the flip side, you have this kind of power to disaggregate, to leverage other clusters to do all that. And that kind of goes against kind of conventional wisdom of how you should do RL infrastructure because conventional wisdom is like you okay.

Speaker 3 | 21:15 - 21:45
You're gonna have this really huge one cluster connected with RDMA, and it's gonna be very expensive, and you're gonna probably spend you know, maybe you're gonna allocate, like, one third to training and two thirds to inference. And sure, if you have very expensive network, it's much easier to copy this one terabyte quickly. But now we have, like, three times larger cluster. Now if your inference engine is more optimized, then maybe you you're gonna save one third of that cluster in terms of GPUs anyway, because you're just more efficient. And you can take, you know, half of this cluster somewhere else in a maybe cheaper hardware in a different region.

Speaker 3 | 21:45 - 21:47
So your cost comes down quite a bit.

Speaker 2 | 21:47 - 21:57
I love that you guys are just grinning as you describe this because it's like, it's so hard, and this is like a systems engineer's dream. Right? And so it's just like a it's an amazing, amazing system you guys have called.

Speaker 1 | 21:57 - 21:59
We spent a bunch of nights working on this. Yeah.

Speaker 2 | 21:59 - 22:11
You look like you spent a long time a lot of time together. What about I mean, you went to the beginning that Kimi is a very large sparse MOE model. Does that make the RL run tricky in any way?

Speaker 1 | 22:11 - 22:12
Mhmm. Yep.

Speaker 2 | 22:12 - 22:12
How so?

Speaker 1 | 22:12 - 22:41
Well, when you do inference, you're essentially doing like a forward pass. It's just kinda like autoregressive. And in this forward pass, it produces like log probabilities of like the tokens it it has sampled. When we ship back the like generations of the model to the trainer, we have to rerun that forward pass because as we mentioned, we are doing asynchronous training. So the model that has produced the pass may have been like actually a few steps behind what the trainer is at.

Speaker 1 | 22:41 - 23:09
And so we have to rerun that forward pass and reproduce log probabilities. Now the problem is in theory, this log probability should be exactly the same if it's the same model version. But even with the same model version, you get slightly or sometimes very different log probability values for the same tokens. So this is often called what, like a numerical mismatch for inference. You hear this about all the time these days.

Speaker 1 | 23:09 - 23:10
For mixture

Speaker 2 | 23:10 - 23:11
Why is that? Why does that happen?

Speaker 3 | 23:11 - 23:19
I mean, primarily because, like, fundamentally floating point arithmetic Yes. Which is doing this is nondeterministic. Yep. So if you Sorry.

Speaker 2 | 23:19 - 23:21
Floating point arithmetic is nondeterministic.

Speaker 3 | 23:21 - 23:32
So, you know, we learned this called it, like, you a plus b plus c. Right? And, like, c plus b plus a, it's gonna be the same result. If you're doing this with integers, with whole numbers on the computer, that's gonna be always true. Yep.

Speaker 3 | 23:32 - 23:57
If you're going to do it with floating point numbers, which are actually like approximate approximation numbers, have this like mantissa and exponents, etcetera. A plus b plus c and c plus b plus a is gonna give you like different results or even like a plus b and b. So basically, fundamentally, it's accumulation order of like all these operations which models do is basically like multiplications and additions. And like addition order matters to your final result. It's all like small differences, but they get amplified through like Exactly.

Speaker 3 | 23:57 - 24:09
Millions and billions of operations. So when you do inference models, usually it doesn't matter that much because you pre train your model. You're actually pretty robust. If you, like, flip some bits, it's still gonna produce you, like, good results. Your benchmarks are not gonna change.

Speaker 3 | 24:09 - 24:42
But RL in particular, because you are using this very, very, like, weak signal to teach the model, the noise from this numerical differences can make or break your training. And that's, like, particularly important. And it it again, it's a interesting intersection between, like, algorithmic and systems part because, you know, you can write a beautiful mess, and it just doesn't work in practice. There are ways how you can drive this difference to pretty much zero. There are always, like, batch invariant ways you can basically, you can be very, very careful and write all your GPU kernels, so they always add numbers in the same order.

Speaker 3 | 24:42 - 24:54
So you always do, like, a plus b plus c and not a different order. It's possible, but that's always has, like, trade offs. Right? Basically, your, like, your system becomes maybe, two x or three x slower. Again, it becomes an interesting trade off.

Speaker 3 | 24:54 - 25:07
Like, okay. What is the 10% of slowdown which we can take? Or in fact, it's actually perfect. Few percent of slowdown we can take to address 90% of this difference. That's, you know, the right trade off, which, you know, we we find together through through iteration.

Speaker 3 | 25:07 - 25:25
And you mentioned that particularly for MOEs and SPARSD is hard. The reason for that is that, like, the way MOEs work is that you will take your activations at every layer, and you would run it through gating layer, which basically decides, okay. For this token, I'm gonna run out of three eighty four experts. I'm gonna run this eight. Right?

Speaker 3 | 25:25 - 25:42
So it's gonna do, like, some mess in, like, top eight scores. Those eight experts gonna be activated. Other ones will not be activated for this token. This operation amplifies your small numerical differences quite a bit because maybe your hidden states were, like, difference by, like, fifth digit after dot. Doesn't really matter.

Speaker 3 | 25:42 - 26:18
But this difference made it so you picked expert number seven versus expert number nine as a kind of as a cutoff. And suddenly, you went and, like, activated totally different part of the model, and your difference got amplified quite a bit. And my models, by definition, are, like, very more sensitive to this mismatch, again, when you do inference or when you do kind of regular load, it usually doesn't matter as a average out. But now if you're trying to base this model learn, this difference is huge because your inference activated expert number seven.
